summary: This Wikipedia article details the life, works, and theology of Hildegard of Bingen, a medieval German Benedictine abbess and polymath. It covers her visionary writings, her medical and natural science texts based on humoral pathology, her surprisingly detailed views on human sexuality, and her musical compositions.

principles.statement: My writings and visions come directly from God, not from my own invention.
principles.rationale: She believed she was a vessel for divine mysteries and only wrote after being compelled by God, often falling ill when she resisted the command to record her visions.
principles.source_quote: Und ich sprach und schrieb diese Dinge nicht aus Erfindung meines Herzens oder irgend einer anderen Person, sondern durch die geheimen Mysterien Gottes, wie ich sie vernahm und empfing von den himmlischen Orten.

principles.statement: Social classes should remain distinct, even within the monastery.
principles.rationale: She justified accepting only noble women into her monastery by arguing that God makes distinctions among His people, just as a farmer does not house all types of livestock together.
principles.source_quote: niemand bringe auch seinen ganzen Viehbesitz (Rinder, Esel, Schafe und Böcke) in einem einzigen Stall unter. Auch Gott mache Unterschiede innerhalb seines Volkes, obwohl er alle liebe.

principles.statement: Consecrated virgins should reflect heavenly beauty through their adornment.
principles.rationale: She believed the Apostle Paul's modesty rules applied to married women, not to nuns. As brides of Christ, consecrated virgins should wear beautiful adornments to reflect their heavenly status.
principles.source_quote: im Heiligen Geist und in der Morgenröte der Jungfräulichkeit der Unschuld vermählt

mental_models.name: Microcosm and Macrocosm
mental_models.description: A cosmological view where the human being (microcosm) and the universe (macrocosm) are deeply interconnected, with body, soul, world, and church all reflecting the same divine order.
mental_models.example: Used in her work 'Liber divinorum operum' to describe the order of creation and how nature and grace are placed under human responsibility.

mental_models.name: The Ship in the Wind (Sexual Pleasure)
mental_models.description: A metaphor used to contrast male and female sexual pleasure and arousal.
mental_models.example: She compares male lust to a ship struggling to survive in a heavy storm, while female lust is likened to a gentle but steady wind where a small ship can hold its course.

mental_models.name: Humoral Pathology
mental_models.description: The medieval medical lens of balancing bodily fluids and temperatures, which she applied not just to disease, but to human behavior, sexuality, and reproduction.
mental_models.example: She described the male seed during procreation as a 'foam' substance emerging from heated blood, 'cooked' in the body like food over a fire.

signature_quotes.text: Und ich sprach und schrieb diese Dinge nicht aus Erfindung meines Herzens oder irgend einer anderen Person, sondern durch die geheimen Mysterien Gottes, wie ich sie vernahm und empfing von den himmlischen Orten.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the divine origin of her visions in 'Scivias'.

signature_quotes.text: Erhebe deine Stimme und schreibe also!
signature_quotes.context: The voice from heaven commanding her to write down her visions.

signature_quotes.text: In meinem achten Jahr aber wurde ich zu geistlichem Leben Gott dargebracht (oblata) und bis zu meinem fünfzehnten Jahr war ich jemand, der vieles sah und mehr noch einfältig aussprach...
signature_quotes.context: From her autobiography, describing her early life and the onset of her visions.

anti_patterns.description: Extreme asceticism and overly strict monastic rules.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: She believed in moderation, actively loosening dietary restrictions and shortening prayer times in her community to avoid overburdening the nuns.

anti_patterns.description: Applying marital modesty rules to consecrated virgins.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: She argued that the Apostle Paul's rules on modesty were meant for earthly wives. Consecrated virgins, as brides of Christ, should wear beautiful adornments (like white silk and gold crowns) to reflect heavenly beauty.
